title: Naive RAG: Chunk, Embed, Retrieve, Stuff
concept: The simplest possible RAG loop and why it works well as a first prototype but breaks down at scale.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of a standard pre-trained LLM when handling custom business-only model when-built, out-of-the-box LLM, you ask questions, an LLM relies solely LLM relies on its pre-trained model?

options.text: They cannot access private data or real-time updates after training.
They are not capable of understanding English sentences.
They can only output numbers, not text.

exercises.explain: Before learning about RAG, we need to know why LLMs need it: they are frozen in time after training and cannot see your private files or live web updates without an external retrieval mechanism.

cards.title: Teach Your LLM New Tricks
cards.body: Large Language Models are smart, but they don't know your private files or the latest news. Naive RAG is the fastest way to feed them your custom data without retraining the whole model.

cards.title: What is Naive RAG?
cards.body: Naive RAG is the basic pipeline where you cut documents into small pieces (chunks), turn them into math patterns (embeddings), search for the pieces closest to a user's question, and paste them into the LLM's prompt.

cards.title: The Index Card Helper
cards.body: Think of Naive RAG like finding a recipe in a massive cookbook by looking at a 1-page index, photocopying the most relevant page, and taping it directly onto your shopping list. You don't read the whole book; you just grab the closest match and stuff it into your workspace.

cards.title: A Quick Match
cards.body: Imagine asking a bot, 'What is our refund policy?' The system searches a database of 100-word handbook snippets, finds the snippet about 'Refunds within 30 days', paste it into the prompt, and the LLM answers correctly.

cards.title: The Prototype Trap
cards.body: People think Naive RAG is production-ready for any task because it's easy to build. In reality, it easily gets confused by messy formatting, duplicate information, or questions that require summarizing entire documents.

cards.title: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
cards.body: Let's compare a basic Naive RAG workflow against a fine-tuning workflow to see how they handle new data.

left.text: Searches external text chunks on the fly and pastes them into the prompt like an open-book exam.

right.text: Changes the internal weights of the model through training, like studying for a closed-book exam.

exercises.prompt: What are the four basic stages of the Naive RAG pipeline in order?
exercises.explain: The four sequential steps of basic RAG are: dividing the text (chunking), converting it to vector numbers (embedding), finding relevant parts (retrieving), and pasting them into the prompt (stuffing).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Naive RAG is the best choice for all enterprise applications because it never makes mistakes or misses context.
exercises.explain: Naive RAG works great for simple search-and-answer tasks on clean data, but often struggles with complex reasoning or huge volumes of messy, unstructured data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe putting retrieved text directly into the model's prompt.
exercises.sentence: The final step of the Naive RAG pipeline is stuffing the retrieved text into the LLM prompt.
exercises.explain: Stuffing is the process of pasting the retrieved document chunks directly into the LLM's prompt window alongside the user's question.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes a Naive RAG system?

options.text: An agent loop that uses Google Search API to find real-time weather and formats it into a neat table.
A script that splits a PDF into 500-character blocks, saves them in ChromaDB, queries them with cosine similarity, and appends the top 3 blocks to the LLM prompt.
A model fine-tuning script that trains a Llama model on customer support transcripts using LoRA.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a classic Naive RAG implementation: it loads a document, chunks it by a simple character count, embeds it, queries it using vector distance, and stuffs the raw text into the system prompt.

exercises.prompt: You build a Naive RAG system to help customer support agents. Why does this simple system fail when a customer asks a complex question that requires connecting clues from Page 2 and Page 85 of a manual?

options.text: It requires upgrading to a much larger LLM size.
Vector databases cannot store more than 100 total chunks.
Information scattered across different pages or chapters is missed or fragmented.
Embedding models automatically erase older text chunks to save space.

exercises.explain: Naive RAG relies entirely on finding matches using raw text chunks. If the answer requires connecting information that is split across two different chapters, a simple vector lookup will fail to retrieve both pieces or contextually stitch them together.
